From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Major war in Central Europe (1618–1648)

For other uses, see [Thirty Years War (disambiguation)](/wiki/Thirty_Years_War_(disambiguation) "Thirty Years War (disambiguation)").

| Thirty Years' War | |
| --- | --- |
| Part of the [European wars of religion](/wiki/European_wars_of_religion "European wars of religion") and [French–Habsburg rivalry](/wiki/French%E2%80%93Habsburg_rivalry "French–Habsburg rivalry") | |
| *The Siege (Defence of a Church Courtyard During the Thirty Years' War)* [Karl Friedrich Lessing](/wiki/Karl_Friedrich_Lessing "Karl Friedrich Lessing"), 1848 | |
| |  |  | | --- | --- | | Date | 23 May 1618 – 24 October 1648 | | Location | [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") | | Result | [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia") | | Territorial changes | * France annexes the [Décapole](/wiki/D%C3%A9capole "Décapole"), and [Sundgau](/wiki/Sundgau "Sundgau")[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECroxton2013225–226-2) * Sweden gains [Wismar](/wiki/Wismar "Wismar"), [Wolin](/wiki/Wolin "Wolin"), [Western Pomerania](/wiki/Western_Pomerania "Western Pomerania"), and [Bremen-Verden](/wiki/Bremen-Verden "Bremen-Verden")[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeitzRischer1995232-3) * [Brandenburg-Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia") obtains [Eastern Pomerania](/wiki/Province_of_Pomerania_(1653-1815) "Province of Pomerania (1653-1815)")[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeitzRischer1995232-3) | | |

The **Thirty Years' War**,[[h]](#cite_note-25) fought primarily in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") between 1618 and 1648, was one of [the most destructive conflicts](/wiki/List_of_wars_by_death_toll "List of wars by death toll") in European history. An estimated 4.5 to 8 million soldiers and civilians died from the effects of battle, famine, or disease, with parts of [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") reporting population declines of over 50%.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997189-26) Related conflicts include the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"), the [War of the Mantuan Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Mantuan_Succession "War of the Mantuan Succession"), the [Franco-Spanish War](/wiki/Franco-Spanish_War_(1635%E2%80%931659) "Franco-Spanish War (1635–1659)"), the [Torstenson War](/wiki/Torstenson_War "Torstenson War"), the [Dutch–Portuguese War](/wiki/Dutch%E2%80%93Portuguese_War "Dutch–Portuguese War"), and the [Portuguese Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War").

Its causes derived from religious conflict within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), sparked by the 16th-century [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"). The 1555 [Peace of Augsburg](/wiki/Peace_of_Augsburg "Peace of Augsburg") attempted to resolve this by dividing the Empire into [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") and [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") states, but was later destabilised by the expansion of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") beyond these boundaries. Combined with disagreements over the limits of imperial authority, religion was thus an important factor in starting the war. However, its scope and extent were largely the consequence of external drivers such as the [French–Habsburg rivalry](/wiki/French%E2%80%93Habsburg_rivalry "French–Habsburg rivalry") and the [Dutch Revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESutherland1992589–590-27)

The war began in [1618](/wiki/Defenestrations_of_Prague "Defenestrations of Prague"),[[i]](#cite_note-28) when the Catholic [Emperor Ferdinand II](/wiki/Emperor_Ferdinand_II "Emperor Ferdinand II") was replaced as [king of Bohemia](/wiki/King_of_Bohemia "King of Bohemia") by the Protestant [Frederick V of the Palatinate](/wiki/Frederick_V_of_the_Palatinate "Frederick V of the Palatinate"). Although Ferdinand quickly [regained control of Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemian_Revolt "Bohemian Revolt"), Frederick's participation expanded fighting [into the Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_campaign "Palatinate campaign"), whose strategic importance drew in the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") and Spain, then engaged in the Eighty Years' War. In addition, the acquisition of territories within the Empire by rulers like [Christian IV of Denmark](/wiki/Christian_IV_of_Denmark "Christian IV of Denmark") and [Gustavus Adolphus](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus "Gustavus Adolphus") of [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire") gave them and other foreign powers an ongoing motive to intervene. Combined with fears that Protestant Christianity in general was threatened, these factors turned an internal dynastic dispute into a European conflict.

The period from 1618 to 1635 was primarily a [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war") within the Holy Roman Empire, which largely ended with the [Peace of Prague](/wiki/Peace_of_Prague_(1635) "Peace of Prague (1635)"). However, [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France")'s entry into the war in [alliance](/wiki/Treaty_of_Compi%C3%A8gne_(1635) "Treaty of Compiègne (1635)") with Sweden turned the empire into one theatre of a wider struggle with their Habsburg rivals, [Emperor Ferdinand III](/wiki/Emperor_Ferdinand_III "Emperor Ferdinand III") and Spain. Fighting ended with the 1648 [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia"), whose terms included greater autonomy for states like [Bavaria](/wiki/Electorate_of_Bavaria "Electorate of Bavaria") and [Saxony](/wiki/Electorate_of_Saxony "Electorate of Saxony"), Swedish territorial gains in northern Germany, as well as acceptance of Dutch independence by Spain. The conflict shifted the [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)") in favour of France and its subsequent expansion under [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV").

## Structural origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structural origins")]

The 16th-century [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") caused [open warfare](/wiki/Schmalkaldic_War "Schmalkaldic War") between [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") and [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics") within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), which ended with the 1552 [Peace of Passau](/wiki/Peace_of_Passau "Peace of Passau"). The [Peace of Augsburg](/wiki/Peace_of_Augsburg "Peace of Augsburg") in 1555 tried to prevent a recurrence by fixing boundaries between the two faiths, using the principle of *[cuius regio, eius religio](/wiki/Cuius_regio,_eius_religio "Cuius regio, eius religio")*. States were designated as either [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), then the most usual form of Protestantism, or Catholic, based on the religion of their ruler. Other provisions protected substantial religious minorities in cities like [Donauwörth](/wiki/Donauw%C3%B6rth "Donauwörth"), and confirmed Lutheran ownership of property taken from the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") since 1552.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker199717–18-29)[[j]](#cite_note-30)

However, the settlement was undermined by the expansion of Protestantism into Catholic areas post-1555, particularly [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), a Protestant doctrine viewed with hostility by both Lutherans and Catholics.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESutherland1992602–603-31) Augsburg also gave individual rulers significantly greater autonomy, allowing larger states to pursue their own objectives, which often clashed with those of central authority or external powers. Conflict sometimes superseded religion, with the Protestant states of [Saxony](/wiki/Electorate_of_Saxony "Electorate of Saxony") and [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia") competing with [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway") and [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire") over the lucrative [Baltic trade](/wiki/Baltic_maritime_trade_(c._1400%E2%80%931800) "Baltic maritime trade (c. 1400–1800)").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193822–24-32)[[k]](#cite_note-34)

Reconciling these differences was hampered by fragmented political institutions, which included 300 [imperial estates](/wiki/Imperial_estate "Imperial estate") distributed across Germany, the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), and present-day France.[[l]](#cite_note-36) These ranged in size and importance from the seven [Prince-electors](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") who voted for the [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"), down to [Prince-bishoprics](/wiki/Prince-bishop "Prince-bishop") and [imperial cities](/wiki/Free_imperial_city "Free imperial city") like [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg").[[m]](#cite_note-37) Each also belonged to a separate regional grouping known as an [Imperial Circle](/wiki/Imperial_Circle "Imperial Circle"), which was chiefly concerned with defence, and operated independently. Above all of these was the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Imperial Diet (Holy Roman Empire)"), which assembled infrequently, and focused on discussion, rather than legislation.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson200917–22-38)

Since 1440, the position of emperor had been held by the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"), the largest single landowner within the Holy Roman Empire. They directly ruled over eight million subjects and territories that included [Austria](/wiki/Archduchy_of_Austria "Archduchy of Austria"), [Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia") and [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193824-39) The Habsburgs also controlled the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") until 1556, when [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") divided his possessions between different branches of the family. This bond was reinforced by frequent inter-marriage, while [Spain](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") retained territories within the Holy Roman Empire such as the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"), [Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan") and [Franche-Comté](/wiki/Franche-Comt%C3%A9 "Franche-Comté").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Vroom_Hendrick_Cornelisz_Dutch_Ships_Ramming_Spanish_Galleys_off_the_Flemish_Coast_in_October_1602.jpg/250px-Vroom_Hendrick_Cornelisz_Dutch_Ships_Ramming_Spanish_Galleys_off_the_Flemish_Coast_in_October_1602.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vroom_Hendrick_Cornelisz_Dutch_Ships_Ramming_Spanish_Galleys_off_the_Flemish_Coast_in_October_1602.jpg)

Dutch ships ramming Spanish galleys in the [Battle of the Narrow Seas](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Narrow_Seas "Battle of the Narrow Seas") during the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"), October 1602

Though the Spanish and Austrian branches often collaborated, they did not generally coordinate their policies because the two entities were very different.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrost1994102-40) Spain was a global maritime superpower, stretching from Europe to the [Philippines](/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines_(1565%E2%80%931898) "History of the Philippines (1565–1898)") and [the Americas](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"), while Austria[[n]](#cite_note-41) was a land-based power, focused on Germany and securing its eastern border against the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938159–161-42) Another key difference was the disparity in relative financial strength, with the [Spanish Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") providing large subsidies to their Austrian counterparts. The loss of these post 1640, as Spain itself struggled with the costs of a long running global war, substantially weakened the imperial position.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrost1994103–105-43)

Prior to the Reformation, shared religion partially compensated for weak imperial institutions. After 1556, rising religious and political tensions allowed states like Lutheran Saxony and Catholic [Bavaria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bavaria "Duchy of Bavaria") to expand their own power, while further weakening imperial authority. This internal political struggle was exacerbated by external powers with their own strategic objectives, such as Spain, the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), or France, confronted by Habsburg lands on its borders to the [north](/wiki/Nord-Pas-de-Calais "Nord-Pas-de-Calais"), [south](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France"), and along the [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees"). Since a number of foreign rulers were also imperial princes, divisions within the empire drew in players like [Christian IV of Denmark](/wiki/Christian_IV_of_Denmark "Christian IV of Denmark"), who joined the war in 1625 as [Duke of Holstein-Gottorp](/wiki/Duke_of_Holstein-Gottorp "Duke of Holstein-Gottorp").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayden19731–23-33)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Background: 1556 to 1618

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Background: 1556 to 1618")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Map_Thirty_Years_War-en.svg/330px-Map_Thirty_Years_War-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Thirty_Years_War-en.svg)

Map of the Thirty Years' War

Disputes occasionally resulted in full-scale conflict like the 1583 to 1588 [Cologne War](/wiki/Cologne_War "Cologne War"), triggered when its [ruler](/wiki/Gebhard_Truchsess_von_Waldburg "Gebhard Truchsess von Waldburg") converted to [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"). More common were events such as the 1606 Battle of the Flags in Donauwörth, when riots broke out after the Lutheran majority blocked a Catholic religious procession. [Emperor Rudolf](/wiki/Emperor_Rudolf "Emperor Rudolf") approved intervention by the Catholic [Maximilian of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian I, Elector of Bavaria"). In return, he was allowed to annex the town, and, as agreed at Augsburg, the official religion changed from Lutheran to Catholic.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009222-44)

When the Imperial Diet opened in February 1608, both Lutherans and Calvinists sought re-confirmation of the Augsburg settlement. In return, the Habsburg heir [Archduke Ferdinand](/wiki/Ferdinand_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand II, Holy Roman Emperor") required the immediate restoration of any property taken from the Catholic Church since 1555, as opposed to the previous practice whereby each case was assessed separately. By threatening all Protestants, his demand paralysed the diet and removed the perception of imperial neutrality.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009224-45)

The loss of faith in central authority meant towns and rulers began strengthening their fortifications and armies, with foreign travellers often commenting on the militarisation of Germany in this period.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker199711-46) When [Frederick IV, Elector Palatine](/wiki/Frederick_IV,_Elector_Palatine "Frederick IV, Elector Palatine") formed the [Protestant Union](/wiki/Protestant_Union "Protestant Union") in 1608, Maximilian responded by setting up the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(German) "Catholic League (German)") in July 1609. Both were largely vehicles for their leaders' dynastic ambitions, but when combined with the 1609 to 1614 [War of the Jülich Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_J%C3%BClich_Succession "War of the Jülich Succession"), the result was to increase tensions throughout the empire.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193847–49-47) Some historians who see the war as primarily a European conflict argue that Jülich marks its beginning, with Spain and Austria backing the Catholic candidate, France and the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") the Protestant.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2008557-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/CaminoEspa%C3%B1ol.svg/250px-CaminoEspa%C3%B1ol.svg.png)](/wiki/File:CaminoEspa%C3%B1ol.svg)

The [Spanish Road](/wiki/Spanish_Road "Spanish Road")   
 **Purple**: [Spanish](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") dependencies   
 **Green**: Ruled by [Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy")   
 **Orange**: Ruled by Spain

External powers became involved in what was an internal German dispute because of the imminent expiry of the 1609 [Twelve Years' Truce](/wiki/Twelve_Years%27_Truce "Twelve Years' Truce"), which suspended the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") between Spain and the Dutch Republic. Before restarting hostilities, [Ambrosio Spinola](/wiki/Ambrosio_Spinola "Ambrosio Spinola"), commander in the Spanish Netherlands, needed to secure the [Spanish Road](/wiki/Spanish_Road "Spanish Road"), an overland route connecting Habsburg possessions in Italy to [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"). This allowed him to move troops and supplies by road, rather than sea where the Dutch navy was dominant; by 1618, the only part not controlled by Spain ran through the [Electoral Palatinate](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193850-49)

Since [Emperor Matthias](/wiki/Emperor_Matthias "Emperor Matthias") had no surviving children, in [July 1617](/wiki/O%C3%B1ate_treaty "Oñate treaty") [Philip III of Spain](/wiki/Philip_III_of_Spain "Philip III of Spain") agreed to support [Ferdinand II](/wiki/Ferdinand_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand II, Holy Roman Emperor")'s election as king of Bohemia and Hungary. In return, Ferdinand made concessions to Spain in northern Italy and Alsace, and agreed to support their offensive against the Dutch. Doing so required his election as emperor, which was not guaranteed; Maximilian of Bavaria, who opposed the increase of Spanish influence in an area he considered his own, tried to create a coalition to support his candidacy.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193863–65-50)

Another option was [Frederick V, Elector Palatine](/wiki/Frederick_V,_Elector_Palatine "Frederick V, Elector Palatine"), a Calvinist who in 1613 married [Elizabeth of Bohemia](/wiki/Elizabeth_of_Bohemia "Elizabeth of Bohemia"), daughter of [James I of England](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"). When Ferdinand was elected king of Bohemia in 1617, he also gained control of its electoral vote, but his conservative Catholicism made him unpopular with the mostly Protestant Bohemian nobility, who were also concerned about the erosion of their rights. Since the seven electors previously consisted of three Protestants and four Catholics, replacing Ferdinand with Frederick would alter the religious balance, making possible the election of a Protestant emperor and the end of Habsburg predominance.[[o]](#cite_note-51) These factors combined to bring about the [Bohemian Revolt](/wiki/Bohemian_Revolt "Bohemian Revolt") in May 1618.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009271–274-52)

### Phase I: 1618 to 1635

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Phase I: 1618 to 1635")]

#### Bohemian Revolt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bohemian Revolt")]

Main article: [Bohemian Revolt](/wiki/Bohemian_Revolt "Bohemian Revolt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Defenestration-prague-1618.jpg/250px-Defenestration-prague-1618.jpg)](/wiki/File:Defenestration-prague-1618.jpg)

A contemporary woodcut depicts the [Third Defenestration of Prague](/wiki/Third_Defenestration_of_Prague "Third Defenestration of Prague") (1618), which marked the beginning of the Bohemian Revolt, and therefore of the first phase of the Thirty Years' War.

Ferdinand once claimed he would rather see his lands destroyed than tolerate [heresy](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy") within them. Less than 18 months after taking control of [Styria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Styria "Duchy of Styria") in 1595, he had eliminated Protestantism in what had been a stronghold of the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBassett201514-53) Absorbed by their war in the Netherlands, his Spanish relatives preferred to avoid antagonising Protestants elsewhere. They recognised the dangers associated with Ferdinand's fervent Catholicism, but supported his claim due to the lack of alternatives.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193874–75-54)

Elected king of Bohemia in May 1617, Ferdinand reconfirmed [Protestant religious freedoms](/wiki/Letter_of_Majesty "Letter of Majesty"), but his record in Styria led to the suspicion he was only awaiting a chance to overturn them. These concerns were heightened after a series of legal disputes over property were all decided in favour of the Catholic Church. In May 1618, Protestant nobles led by [Count Thurn](/wiki/Jind%C5%99ich_Maty%C3%A1%C5%A1_Thurn "Jindřich Matyáš Thurn") met in [Prague Castle](/wiki/Prague_Castle "Prague Castle") with Ferdinand's two Catholic representatives, [Vilem Slavata](/wiki/Vilem_Slavata_of_Chlum "Vilem Slavata of Chlum") and [Jaroslav Borzita](/wiki/Jaroslav_Borzita_of_Martinice "Jaroslav Borzita of Martinice"). In what became known as the [Third Defenestration of Prague](/wiki/Third_Defenestration_of_Prague "Third Defenestration of Prague"), both men were thrown out of the castle windows along with their secretary [Filip Fabricius](/wiki/Filip_Fabricius "Filip Fabricius"), although all three survived.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193878–79-55)

Thurn established a Protestant-dominated government in Bohemia, while unrest expanded into [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") and the Habsburg heartlands of [Lower](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria") and [Upper Austria](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria"), where much of the nobility was also Protestant. Losing control of these threatened the entire Habsburg state, while in addition to its crucial electoral vote, Bohemia was one of the most prosperous areas of the Empire. Regaining control was vital for the Austrian Habsburgs, but chronic financial weakness left them dependent on Maximilian and Spain for the resources needed to achieve this.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBassett201512,_15-56)

Spanish involvement in Bohemia drew in the Dutch, and also the potential involvements of the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") or [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"),[[42]](#cite_note-57) although the strongly Catholic [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII") faced his own domestic [Protestant rebellion](/wiki/Huguenot_rebellions "Huguenot rebellions") and refused to support them elsewhere. The revolt also provided opportunities for external opponents of the Habsburgs, including the [Savoyard state](/wiki/Savoyard_state "Savoyard state"). Funded by Frederick and [Charles Emmanuel I, Duke of Savoy](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_I,_Duke_of_Savoy "Charles Emmanuel I, Duke of Savoy"), a mercenary army under [Ernst von Mansfeld](/wiki/Ernst_von_Mansfeld "Ernst von Mansfeld") was sent to support the Bohemian rebels. Attempts by Maximilian and John George of Saxony to broker a negotiated solution ended when Emperor Matthias died in March 1619, since many believed the loss of his authority and influence had fatally damaged the Habsburgs.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193881–82-58)

By mid-June 1619, an army led by Thurn was outside [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), and despite defeating Mansfeld at [Sablat](/wiki/Battle_of_Sablat "Battle of Sablat"), Ferdinand's position continued to worsen.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193894-59) [Gabriel Bethlen](/wiki/Gabriel_Bethlen "Gabriel Bethlen"), Calvinist [Prince of Transylvania](/wiki/Prince_of_Transylvania "Prince of Transylvania"), invaded Hungary with Ottoman support, although war with [Poland in 1620](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Ottoman_War_(1620%E2%80%9321) "Polish–Ottoman War (1620–21)"), followed by the [1623 to 1639 conflict with Persia](/wiki/Ottoman%E2%80%93Safavid_War_(1623%E2%80%931639) "Ottoman–Safavid War (1623–1639)"), prevented their direct participation.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaramova2014121–122-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Bohemian-palatinate_phase_En_Map.svg/330px-Bohemian-palatinate_phase_En_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bohemian-palatinate_phase_En_Map.svg)

The Bohemian-Palatinate phase

On 19 August, the Bohemian Estates rescinded Ferdinand's 1617 election as king and a week later formally offered the crown to Frederick. On 28 August, Ferdinand was elected Holy Roman Emperor, making war inevitable if Frederick accepted the Bohemian crown. Most of Frederick's advisors urged him to reject it, as did the Duke of Savoy, and his father-in-law James I.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193898–99-61)

However, he received backing from [Christian of Anhalt](/wiki/Christian_I,_Prince_of_Anhalt-Bernburg "Christian I, Prince of Anhalt-Bernburg"), along with [Maurice of Orange](/wiki/Maurice,_Prince_of_Orange "Maurice, Prince of Orange"), for whom conflict in Germany was a means to divert Spanish resources from the Netherlands. The Dutch offered subsidies to Frederick and the Protestant Union, helped raise loans for Bohemia, and provided weapons and munitions.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995b469-62) However, wider European support failed to materialise, largely due to lack of enthusiasm for removing a legally elected ruler, regardless of religion.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood193898–99-61)

Although Frederick accepted the crown and entered Prague in October 1619, his support eroded over the next few months. In July 1620, the Protestant Union proclaimed its neutrality, while John George of Saxony backed Ferdinand in return for the cession of [Lusatia](/wiki/Lusatia "Lusatia") and a guarantee of Lutheran rights in Bohemia. Maximilian of Bavaria funded a combined [Imperial](/wiki/Imperial_Army_of_the_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Imperial Army of the Holy Roman Emperor")-Catholic League army led by [Count Tilly](/wiki/Johann_Tserclaes,_Count_of_Tilly "Johann Tserclaes, Count of Tilly") and [de Bucquoy](/wiki/Charles_Bonaventure_de_Longueval,_2nd_Count_of_Bucquoy "Charles Bonaventure de Longueval, 2nd Count of Bucquoy"), which [pacified](/wiki/Pacification_(military_action) "Pacification (military action)") Upper and Lower Austria and occupied western Bohemia before marching on Prague. Defeated at the [Battle of White Mountain](/wiki/Battle_of_White_Mountain "Battle of White Mountain") in November 1620, the Bohemian army disintegrated, and Frederick fled the country.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938127–129-63)

#### Palatinate campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Palatinate campaign")]

Main article: [Palatinate campaign](/wiki/Palatinate_campaign "Palatinate campaign")

By abandoning Frederick, the German princes hoped to restrict the dispute to Bohemia, an objective thwarted by Maximilian's dynastic ambitions. In the October 1619 [Treaty of Munich](/wiki/Treaty_of_Munich_(1619) "Treaty of Munich (1619)"), Ferdinand transferred the Palatinate's electoral vote to Bavaria, and allowed Maximilian to annex the [Upper Palatinate](/wiki/Upper_Palatinate "Upper Palatinate").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStutler201437–38-64) Many Protestants had supported Ferdinand because in principle they opposed the deposition of a legally elected ruler, and now objected to Frederick's removal on the same grounds. For Catholics, it presented an opportunity to regain lands and properties lost since 1555, a combination which destabilised large parts of the Empire.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938117-65)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Joachim_von_Sandrart_-_Maximilian_I%2C_Elector_of_Bavaria.jpg/250px-Joachim_von_Sandrart_-_Maximilian_I%2C_Elector_of_Bavaria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joachim_von_Sandrart_-_Maximilian_I,_Elector_of_Bavaria.jpg)

[Maximilian I, Elector of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian I, Elector of Bavaria") whose seizure of the Palatinate expanded the war

At the same time, Spain was drawn into the conflict due to the strategic importance of the Spanish Road for their war in the Netherlands, and its proximity to the Palatinate. When an army led by [Córdoba](/wiki/Gonzalo_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_C%C3%B3rdoba_(1585%E2%80%931635) "Gonzalo Fernández de Córdoba (1585–1635)") occupied the [Lower Palatinate](/wiki/Lower_Palatinate "Lower Palatinate") in October 1619, James I sent English naval forces against Spanish colonial possessions and threatened war if Spanish troops were not withdrawn by spring 1621. These actions were primarily designed to placate his opponents in [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), who considered his pro-Spanish policy a betrayal of the Protestant cause.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaller1974147–148-66) Spanish chief minister [Olivares](/wiki/Gaspar_de_Guzm%C3%A1n,_Count-Duke_of_Olivares "Gaspar de Guzmán, Count-Duke of Olivares") correctly interpreted them as an invitation to open negotiations, and in return for an Anglo-Spanish alliance offered to restore Frederick to his Rhineland possessions.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaller1974152–154-67)

Since Frederick's demand for full restitution of his lands and titles was incompatible with the Treaty of Munich, hopes of a negotiated peace quickly evaporated. Despite defeat in Bohemia, Frederick's allies included [Georg Friedrich of Baden](/wiki/George_Frederick,_Margrave_of_Baden-Durlach "George Frederick, Margrave of Baden-Durlach") and [Christian of Brunswick](/wiki/Christian_the_Younger_of_Brunswick "Christian the Younger of Brunswick"), while the Dutch provided him with military support after the Eighty Years' War restarted in April 1621 and his father-in-law James funded an army of mercenaries under Mansfeld. However, their failure to co-ordinate effectively led to a series of defeats by Spanish and Catholic League forces, at [Wimpfen](/wiki/Battle_of_Wimpfen "Battle of Wimpfen") in May 1622, which forced Georg Friedrich to seek peace and at [Höchst](/wiki/Battle_of_H%C3%B6chst "Battle of Höchst") in June, which forced Christian to meet up with Mansfeld. By November 1622, Spanish and imperial troops controlled most of the Palatinate, apart from [Frankenthal](/wiki/Siege_of_Frankenthal "Siege of Frankenthal"), which was held by a small English garrison under Sir [Horace Vere](/wiki/Horace_Vere "Horace Vere"). Mansfeld and the remnants of his army fled to the Dutch Republic along with Frederick, who lived in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") until his death in November 1632.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielvogel2017447-68)

When the Imperial Diet met in February 1623, Ferdinand forced through provisions transferring Frederick's titles, lands, and electoral vote to Maximilian. He did so with support from the Catholic League, despite strong opposition from Protestant members, as well as the Spanish. The Palatinate was clearly lost, and in March Vere surrendered Frankenthal, while Tilly's victory over Christian of Brunswick at [Stadtlohn](/wiki/Battle_of_Stadtlohn "Battle of Stadtlohn") in August completed military operations.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPursell2003182–185-69) However, Spanish and Dutch involvement in the campaign was a significant step in internationalising the war, while Frederick's removal meant other Protestant princes began discussing armed resistance to preserve their own rights and territories.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938162–164-70)

#### Danish intervention (1625–1629)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Danish intervention (1625–1629)")]

Key locations, 1625–1629 on the map of the modern federal state of [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony")

With Saxony dominating the [Upper Saxon Circle](/wiki/Upper_Saxon_Circle "Upper Saxon Circle") and [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg") the [Lower](/wiki/Lower_Saxon_Circle "Lower Saxon Circle"), both *kreise* remained neutral in the early stages. However, Frederick's deposition in 1623 united the Lutheran John George of Saxony and Calvinist [George William, Elector of Brandenburg](/wiki/George_William,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "George William, Elector of Brandenburg"), who became concerned Ferdinand intended to reclaim formerly Catholic bishoprics now held by Protestants. These fears seemed confirmed in early 1625 when Tilly returned to the Catholic church properties in [Halberstadt](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Halberstadt "Roman Catholic Diocese of Halberstadt") which had been Protestant since 1566.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938179–181-71)

As Duke of Holstein, Christian IV was also a member of the Lower Saxon circle, while the Danish economy relied on the Baltic trade and tolls from traffic through the [Øresund](/wiki/%C3%98resund "Øresund").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockhart2007107–109-72) In 1621, Hamburg accepted Danish "supervision", while his son [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_III_of_Denmark "Frederick III of Denmark") became joint-administrator of [Lübeck](/wiki/Prince-bishopric_of_L%C3%BCbeck "Prince-bishopric of Lübeck"), [Bremen](/wiki/Prince-Archbishopric_of_Bremen "Prince-Archbishopric of Bremen"), and [Verden](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Verden "Prince-Bishopric of Verden"); possession ensured Danish control of the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe") and [Weser](/wiki/Weser "Weser") rivers.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdoch200053-73)

Ferdinand had paid [Albrecht von Wallenstein](/wiki/Albrecht_von_Wallenstein "Albrecht von Wallenstein") for his support against Frederick with estates confiscated from the Bohemian rebels, and now contracted with him to conquer the north on a similar basis. In May 1625, the Lower Saxony *kreis* elected Christian their military commander, although not without resistance; Saxony and Brandenburg viewed Denmark and Sweden as competitors, and wanted to avoid either becoming involved in the empire. Attempts to negotiate a peaceful solution failed as the conflict in Germany became part of the wider struggle between France and their Habsburg rivals in Spain and Austria.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009387-10)

In the June 1624 [Treaty of Compiègne](/wiki/Treaty_of_Compi%C3%A8gne_(1624) "Treaty of Compiègne (1624)"), France had agreed to subsidise the Dutch war against Spain for a minimum of three years, while in the December 1625 [Treaty of The Hague](/wiki/Treaty_of_The_Hague_(1625) "Treaty of The Hague (1625)"), the Dutch and English agreed to finance Danish intervention in the Empire.[[p]](#cite_note-75) Hoping to create a wider coalition against Ferdinand, the Dutch invited France, Sweden, Savoy, Transylvania, and the [Republic of Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") to join, but it was overtaken by events.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavenport1917295-76) In early 1626, [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu"), main architect of the alliance, faced a new Huguenot rebellion at home and in the March [Treaty of Monzón](/wiki/Treaty_of_Monz%C3%B3n "Treaty of Monzón"), France withdrew from northern Italy and the critical [Valtellina Pass](/wiki/Valtellina "Valtellina") , re-opening the Spanish Road.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938208-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Danish_phase_En_Map.svg/250px-Danish_phase_En_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Danish_phase_En_Map.svg)

Danish intervention

Dutch and English subsidies enabled Christian to devise an ambitious three part campaign plan. While he led the main force down the Weser, Mansfeld would attack Wallenstein in [Magdeburg](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Magdeburg "Archbishopric of Magdeburg"), supported by forces led by Christian of Brunswick and [Maurice of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Maurice,_Landgrave_of_Hesse-Kassel "Maurice, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel"). However, Mansfeld was defeated at [Dessau Bridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Dessau_Bridge "Battle of Dessau Bridge") in April, and when Maurice refused to support him, Christian of Brunswick fell back to [Wolfenbüttel](/wiki/Wolfenb%C3%BCttel "Wolfenbüttel"), where he died of disease shortly after. The Danes were comprehensively beaten at [Lutter](/wiki/Battle_of_Lutter "Battle of Lutter") in August, and Mansfeld's army retreated south to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), where his army dissolved after his death in November.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938212-78)

Many of Christian's German allies, such as [Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Landgraviate_of_Hesse-Kassel "Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel"), had little interest in replacing imperial domination with Danish, while few of the Dutch or English subsidies were ever paid. [Charles I of England](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") allowed Christian to recruit up to 9,000 Scottish mercenaries, but they took time to arrive, and although able to slow Wallenstein's advance, were insufficient to stop him.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdochGrosjean201443–44-79) By the end of 1627, Wallenstein had occupied [Mecklenburg](/wiki/Mecklenburg "Mecklenburg"), [Pomerania](/wiki/Capitulation_of_Franzburg "Capitulation of Franzburg"), and [Jutland](/wiki/Jutland "Jutland"), and began making plans to construct a fleet capable of challenging Danish control of the Baltic. He was supported by Spain, for whom it provided an opportunity to open another front against the Dutch.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009426-80)

On 13 May 1628, his deputy [von Arnim](/wiki/Hans_Georg_von_Arnim-Boitzenburg "Hans Georg von Arnim-Boitzenburg") besieged [Stralsund](/wiki/Siege_of_Stralsund_(1628) "Siege of Stralsund (1628)"), the only port with facilities large enough to build this fleet. [Gustavus Adolphus](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus "Gustavus Adolphus") responded by sending several thousand Scots to Stralsund under [Alexander Leslie](/wiki/Alexander_Leslie "Alexander Leslie"), who was also appointed governor.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdochGrosjean201448–49-81) Von Arnim abandoned the siege on 4 August, but three weeks later Christian suffered another defeat at [Wolgast](/wiki/Battle_of_Wolgast "Battle of Wolgast"). He began negotiations with Wallenstein, who despite his recent victories was concerned by the prospect of Swedish intervention, and thus anxious to make peace.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockhart2007170-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Albrecht_von_Wallenstein.jpeg/250px-Albrecht_von_Wallenstein.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Albrecht_von_Wallenstein.jpeg)

[Albrecht von Wallenstein](/wiki/Albrecht_von_Wallenstein "Albrecht von Wallenstein") achieved great military success for the Empire but his power threatened both Ferdinand and the German princes.

With Ferdinand's resources stretched by the outbreak of the [War of the Mantuan Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Mantuan_Succession "War of the Mantuan Succession"), Wallenstein persuaded him to agree relatively lenient terms in the June 1629 [Treaty of Lübeck](/wiki/Treaty_of_L%C3%BCbeck "Treaty of Lübeck"). These allowed Christian to retain [Schleswig](/wiki/Duchy_of_Schleswig "Duchy of Schleswig") and Holstein in return for relinquishing Bremen and Verden, and abandoning support for the German Protestants. While Denmark kept Schleswig and Holstein until [1864](/wiki/Second_Schleswig_War "Second Schleswig War"), this effectively ended its period as a major power in the Baltic region.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockhart2007172-83)

Once again, the methods used to obtain victory explain why the war failed to end. Ferdinand's chronic financial weakness meant Wallenstein raised money by demanding ransoms from towns, while his men plundered the local territory, regardless of whether it belonged to allies or opponents. When Ferdinand deposed the hereditary [Duke of Mecklenburg](/wiki/Adolf_Frederick_I,_Duke_of_Mecklenburg "Adolf Frederick I, Duke of Mecklenburg") in early 1628 and appointed Wallenstein in his place, the act united all German princes in opposition, regardless of religion. However, Maximilian's desire to retain the Palatinate meant the Catholic League argued only for a return to the position prevailing before 1627, while Protestants wanted that of 1618.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938232–233-84)

Made overconfident by success, in March 1629, Ferdinand passed an [Edict of Restitution](/wiki/Edict_of_Restitution "Edict of Restitution"), which required all lands taken from the Catholic church after 1555 to be returned. While technically legal, politically it was extremely unwise, since doing so would alter nearly every single state boundary in north and central Germany, deny the existence of Calvinism and restore Catholicism in areas where it had not been a significant presence for nearly a century. Well aware none of the princes involved would agree, Ferdinand used the device of an imperial [edict](/wiki/Edict "Edict"), once again asserting his right to alter laws without consultation. This new assault on "German liberties" ensured continuing opposition and undermined his previous success.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938242–244-85)

At the same time, his Spanish allies were reluctant to antagonise German Protestants as the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") had now shifted in favour of the Dutch Republic. Madrid's financial position steadily deteriorated in the 1620s, particularly after the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") captured the [Spanish treasure fleet](/wiki/Spanish_treasure_fleet "Spanish treasure fleet") at [Matanzas](/wiki/Battle_in_the_Bay_of_Matanzas "Battle in the Bay of Matanzas") in 1628. Fighting in Italy diverted Spanish resources from the Netherlands,[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995b497-86) allowing [Frederick Henry](/wiki/Frederick_Henry,_Prince_of_Orange "Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange") to [besiege 's-Hertogenbosch](/wiki/Siege_of_%27s-Hertogenbosch "Siege of 's-Hertogenbosch") in 1629. [Imperial intervention](/wiki/Invasion_of_the_Veluwe_(1629) "Invasion of the Veluwe (1629)") could not prevent its fall, further the losses of [Oldenzaal](/wiki/Siege_of_Oldenzaal_(1626) "Siege of Oldenzaal (1626)") and [Groenlo](/wiki/Siege_of_Groenlo_(1627) "Siege of Groenlo (1627)") in 1626 and 1627 respectively, ended the Spanish hope to cut off the Dutch from the East.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995b511-87)

#### Sweden invades Germany (1630–1635)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sweden invades Germany (1630–1635)")]

Main article: [Swedish intervention in the Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Swedish_intervention_in_the_Thirty_Years%27_War "Swedish intervention in the Thirty Years' War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Strasbourg_walter_gustave_adolphe.JPG/250px-Strasbourg_walter_gustave_adolphe.JPG)](/wiki/File:Strasbourg_walter_gustave_adolphe.JPG)

[Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus_of_Sweden "Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden") at [Breitenfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Breitenfeld_(1631) "Battle of Breitenfeld (1631)") in 1631

From 1626 to 1629, Gustavus was engaged in a [war with Poland–Lithuania](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Swedish_War_(1626%E2%80%931629) "Polish–Swedish War (1626–1629)"), ruled by his Catholic cousin [Sigismund](/wiki/Sigismund_III_Vasa "Sigismund III Vasa"), who also claimed the Swedish throne and was backed by Ferdinand II. Once this conflict ended, and with only a few minor states like [Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Landgraviate_of_Hesse-Kassel "Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel") still openly opposing Ferdinand, Gustavus became an obvious ally for Richelieu.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaland198098–99-88) In September 1629, the latter helped negotiate the [Truce of Altmark](/wiki/Truce_of_Altmark "Truce of Altmark") between Sweden and Poland, freeing Gustavus to enter the war. Partly a genuine desire to support his Protestant co-religionists, like Christian he also wanted to maximize his share of the Baltic trade that provided much of Sweden's income.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938385–386-89)

After diplomatic negotiations with Ferdinand II failed, Gustavus landed in [Pomerania](/wiki/Duchy_of_Pomerania "Duchy of Pomerania") in June 1630 with nearly 18,000 troops. Using Stralsund as a bridgehead, he marched south along the [Oder](/wiki/Oder "Oder") towards [Stettin](/wiki/Szczecin "Szczecin") and coerced [Bogislaw XIV, Duke of Pomerania](/wiki/Bogislaw_XIV,_Duke_of_Pomerania "Bogislaw XIV, Duke of Pomerania"), into agreeing an [alliance](/wiki/Treaty_of_Stettin_(1630) "Treaty of Stettin (1630)") which secured his interests in Pomerania against his rival Sigismund.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorrhem201928–29-90) As a result, the Poles turned their attention to Russia, who initiated the 1632 to 1634 [Smolensk War](/wiki/Smolensk_War "Smolensk War").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorshnev1995106-91)

However, Swedish expectations of widespread German support proved unrealistic. By the end of 1630, their only new ally was the Administrator of Magdeburg, [Christian William](/wiki/Christian_William_of_Brandenburg "Christian William of Brandenburg") whose capital was under siege by Tilly.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997120-92) Despite the devastation inflicted by Wallenstein's soldiers, Saxony and Brandenburg were ambivalent about Swedish intervention. Both states had their own ambitions in Pomerania, while experience showed inviting external powers into the Empire was easier than getting them to leave.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Connell1968253–254-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Sack_of_Magdeburg_1631.jpg/250px-Sack_of_Magdeburg_1631.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sack_of_Magdeburg_1631.jpg)

The [Sack of Magdeburg](/wiki/Sack_of_Magdeburg "Sack of Magdeburg") in 1631

Gustavus put pressure on Brandenburg by sacking Küstrin and [Frankfurt an der Oder](/wiki/Battle_of_Frankfurt_an_der_Oder "Battle of Frankfurt an der Oder") and then bombarding George William in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), while the [Sack of Magdeburg](/wiki/Sack_of_Magdeburg "Sack of Magdeburg") in May 1631 provided a powerful warning of the consequences of imperial victory.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997128-94) Once again, Richelieu used French financial power to bridge differences between the Swedes and their potential allies. In the 1631 [Treaty of Bärwalde](/wiki/Treaty_of_B%C3%A4rwalde "Treaty of Bärwalde"), he agreed to fund the Swedes and a number of small including Hesse-Kassel, Saxony, and Brandenburg.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Connell1968256-95) These payments amounted to 400,000 [Reichstaler](/wiki/Reichstaler "Reichstaler") per year, or one million [livres](/wiki/French_livre "French livre"), enabling Sweden to support a total of 36,000 troops.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorshnev199538-96)

With this army, Gustavus invaded southern Germany, winning victories at [Breitenfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Breitenfeld_(1631) "Battle of Breitenfeld (1631)") in September 1631, then [Rain](/wiki/Battle_of_Rain "Battle of Rain") in April 1632, where Tilly was killed.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938305–306-97) However, Wallenstein realised the Swedes were overextended, and established himself at [Fürth](/wiki/F%C3%BCrth "Fürth"), across their supply lines to the Baltic. At the [Battle of the Alte Veste](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Alte_Veste "Battle of the Alte Veste") in late August, a failed Swedish assault on the Imperial camp outside the town was bloodily repulsed, arguably the greatest blunder committed by Gustavus during his German campaign.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrzezinski20014-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Swedish_phase_map.svg/250px-Swedish_phase_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Swedish_phase_map.svg)

Campaigns during the Swedish phase

Two months later, the Swedes and Imperialists met at [Lützen](/wiki/Battle_of_L%C3%BCtzen_(1632) "Battle of Lützen (1632)"), where both sides suffered heavy casualties. Gustavus himself was killed and some Swedish units incurred losses of over 60%.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson201889-99) Fighting continued until dusk when Wallenstein retreated, abandoning his artillery and wounded.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson201889-99) Despite the loss of Gustavus, most commentators consider the battle a Swedish victory, although the result continues to be disputed.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson201899-100)[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrzezinski200174-101)

After his death, Gustavus' policies were continued by his Chancellor [Axel Oxenstierna](/wiki/Axel_Oxenstierna "Axel Oxenstierna"), and [Bernard of Saxe-Weimar](/wiki/Bernard_of_Saxe-Weimar "Bernard of Saxe-Weimar") and [Gustav Horn](/wiki/Gustav_Horn,_Count_of_Pori "Gustav Horn, Count of Pori"). With French backing, Sweden and a number of smaller German states formed the [Heilbronn League](/wiki/Heilbronn_League "Heilbronn League") in April 1633, although [Brunswick-Lüneburg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brunswick-L%C3%BCneburg "Duchy of Brunswick-Lüneburg"), Saxony, and Brandenburg remained outside. In July, the Swedes and their allies defeated a Bavarian army at [Oldendorf](/wiki/Battle_of_Oldendorf "Battle of Oldendorf").[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009523-102) Wallenstein's critics claimed this was due to his failure to support them, while rumours spread that he was preparing to switch sides. As a result, Ferdinand II ordered his arrest in February 1634, and [he was assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_Wallenstein "Assassination of Wallenstein") by his own officers in Cheb on 25th.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938220–223-103)

The loss of Wallenstein and his organisation left Ferdinand II reliant on Spain for military support. Since their main concern was to re-open the Spanish Road for their campaign against the Dutch, the focus of the war now shifted from the north to the Rhineland and Bavaria. [Cardinal-Infante Ferdinand of Austria](/wiki/Cardinal-Infante_Ferdinand_of_Austria "Cardinal-Infante Ferdinand of Austria"), new Governor of the Spanish Netherlands, raised an army of 18,000 in Italy, which met up with 15,000 Imperial troops at Donauwörth on 2 September 1634. Four days later, their combined army defeated Horn and Bernard at [Nördlingen](/wiki/Battle_of_N%C3%B6rdlingen_(1634) "Battle of Nördlingen (1634)"), a result that cost Sweden control of southern Germany.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamen2003385–386-104)

### Phase II: French intervention, 1635 to 1648

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Phase II: French intervention, 1635 to 1648")]

See also: [Franco-Spanish War (1635–1659)](/wiki/Franco-Spanish_War_(1635%E2%80%931659) "Franco-Spanish War (1635–1659)")

By triggering direct French intervention, Nördlingen expanded the conflict rather than ending it. Richelieu provided the Swedes with [new subsidies](/wiki/Treaty_of_Compi%C3%A8gne_(1635) "Treaty of Compiègne (1635)"), hired mercenaries led by [Bernard of Saxe-Weimar](/wiki/Bernard_of_Saxe-Weimar "Bernard of Saxe-Weimar") for an offensive in the Rhineland, and in May 1635 initiated the [Franco-Spanish War (1635–1659)](/wiki/Franco-Spanish_War_(1635%E2%80%931659) "Franco-Spanish War (1635–1659)").[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997132–134-105) A few days later, the German states and Ferdinand agreed to the [Peace of Prague](/wiki/Peace_of_Prague_(1635) "Peace of Prague (1635)"). In return for withdrawing the Edict of Restitution, the Heilbronn and Catholic Leagues agreed to dissolve their forces, which were replaced by a single Imperial army, although Saxony and Bavaria retained control of their own forces. This is generally seen as the point when the war ceased to be a primarily inter-German religious conflict.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBireley197632-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Cardinal_de_Richelieu_%28detail%29.jpg/250px-Cardinal_de_Richelieu_%28detail%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cardinal_de_Richelieu_(detail).jpg)

[Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu"), French chief minister from 1624 until 1642, and creator of the anti-Habsburg alliance

In March 1635, French soldiers entered the [Valtellina](/wiki/Valtellina "Valtellina"), cutting the link between Milan and the Empire.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamen2003387-107) This was followed in May by an invasion of the Spanish Netherlands, which collapsed with the loss of 17,000 men. In [March 1636](/wiki/Treaty_of_Wismar "Treaty of Wismar"), France joined the Thirty Years' War as an ally of Sweden, which was increasingly reliant on French financing for its continued participation.[[q]](#cite_note-109) The Spanish responded by invading northern France, causing panic in Paris before lack of supplies forced them to retreat.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995a272–273-110) Elsewhere, Saxony began the year by occupying Pomerania, before defeats at [Dömitz](/wiki/Battle_of_D%C3%B6mitz "Battle of Dömitz") and [Wittstock](/wiki/Battle_of_Wittstock "Battle of Wittstock") in October re-established Swedish predominance in northeast Germany.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdochZickermannMarks201280–85-111)

Ferdinand II died in February 1637, and was succeeded by his son [Ferdinand III](/wiki/Ferdinand_III,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand III, Holy Roman Emperor"), who faced a precarious military position. Although [Matthias Gallas](/wiki/Matthias_Gallas "Matthias Gallas") had forced [Johan Banér](/wiki/Johan_Ban%C3%A9r "Johan Banér"), the new Swedish commander, back to the Baltic, in March 1638 Bernard of Saxe Weimar destroyed an Imperial army at [Rheinfelden](/wiki/Battle_of_Rheinfelden "Battle of Rheinfelden"). His capture of [Breisach](/wiki/Battle_of_Breisach "Battle of Breisach") in December severed the Spanish Road, while [Charles I Louis](/wiki/Charles_I_Louis "Charles I Louis") raised an army to regain his father's possessions in the Palatinate. Although the latter was routed by [von Hatzfeldt](/wiki/Melchior_von_Hatzfeldt "Melchior von Hatzfeldt") at [Vlotho](/wiki/Battle_of_Vlotho "Battle of Vlotho") in October, lack of supplies obliged Gallas to withdraw from the Baltic.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009595–598-112)

In April 1639, Banér defeated the Saxons at [Chemnitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Chemnitz "Battle of Chemnitz"), then entered Bohemia in May.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009615-113) To retrieve the situation, Ferdinand diverted [Piccolomini](/wiki/Ottavio_Piccolomini "Ottavio Piccolomini")'s army from [Thionville](/wiki/Relief_of_Thionville "Relief of Thionville"), ending direct military cooperation between Austria and Spain.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009661–662-114) Pressure grew on Olivares to make peace, especially after French and Swedish gains in Germany cut the Spanish Road, forcing [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") to resupply their armies in Flanders by sea. Attempts to re-assert maritime control ended when the [Dutch fleet](/wiki/Dutch_States_Navy "Dutch States Navy") under [Maarten Tromp](/wiki/Maarten_Tromp "Maarten Tromp") won a significant victory over the Spanish at the [Downs](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Downs "Battle of the Downs") in October 1639.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBely201494–95-115)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995b537-116)

The French occupied Spanish-controlled [Artois](/wiki/Artois "Artois") in 1640, while [Dutch attacks](/wiki/Dutch%E2%80%93Portuguese_War "Dutch–Portuguese War") on [Portuguese colonies](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), combined with opposition to taxes, led to revolts in both [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War") and [Catalonia](/wiki/Reapers%27_War "Reapers' War").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta20054-117) Olivares now argued Spain should accept Dutch independence, and focus on preventing further French gains in the Spanish Netherlands.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Gelderen2002284-118) This appeared achievable since most of the Dutch *[regenten](/wiki/Regenten "Regenten")* believed the war was won, the only question being the price of peace. They therefore reduced the army budget for 1640, despite objections from Frederick Henry.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlgraAlgra1956120-119)

Key locations 1635 to 1648 mentioned in text

After Bernard died in July 1639, his troops joined Banér's Swedish army in an ineffectual campaign along the Weser, the highlight being a surprise attack in January 1641 on the Imperial Diet in Regensburg.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997150-121) Forced to retreat, Banér reached [Halberstadt](/wiki/Halberstadt "Halberstadt") in May where he died, and despite beating off an Imperial force at [Wolfenbüttel](/wiki/Battle_of_Wolfenb%C3%BCttel_(1641) "Battle of Wolfenbüttel (1641)") in June, his largely German troops mutinied due to lack of pay.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938446-122) The situation was saved by the arrival of [Lennart Torstensson](/wiki/Lennart_Torstensson "Lennart Torstensson") in November with 7,000 Swedish recruits and enough cash to satisfy the mutineers.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938447-123)

French victory at [Kempen](/wiki/Battle_of_Kempen "Battle of Kempen") in January 1642 was followed by [Second Breitenfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Breitenfeld_(1642) "Battle of Breitenfeld (1642)") in October 1642, where Torstensson inflicted almost 10,000 casualties on an Imperial army led by [Archduke Leopold Wilhelm of Austria](/wiki/Archduke_Leopold_Wilhelm_of_Austria "Archduke Leopold Wilhelm of Austria").[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClodfelter200841-124) The Swedes captured [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig") in December, although they failed to take [Freiberg](/wiki/Freiberg "Freiberg"),[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009636–639-125) and by 1643 the Saxon army had been reduced to a few isolated garrisons.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009641–642-126) Despite these setbacks, Ferdinand fought on, hoping to improve his position enough to exclude the Imperial Estates from his peace negotiations with France and Sweden, and allow him to represent the Empire as a whole.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiltonAxworthySimms201860–65-127)

This seemed more likely when Richelieu died in December 1642, followed by [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII") in May 1643, leaving his five-year-old son [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") as king. However, Richelieu's policies were continued by his successor [Cardinal Mazarin](/wiki/Cardinal_Mazarin "Cardinal Mazarin"), while gains in Alsace allowed France to focus on the war against Spain. In 1643, the Army of Flanders invaded northern France, but were decisively beaten by [Condé](/wiki/Louis_II_de_Bourbon,_Prince_de_Cond%C3%A9 "Louis II de Bourbon, Prince de Condé") at [Rocroi](/wiki/Battle_of_Rocroi "Battle of Rocroi") on 19 May.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997154-128) This ended any prospect of re-opening the Spanish Road, and Madrid finally accepted the reality of Dutch independence.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997171-129)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Tortensson1642_marcossouza.jpg/250px-Tortensson1642_marcossouza.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tortensson1642_marcossouza.jpg)

Campaigns during the Franco-Swedish phase (until 1642)

However, Condé was unable to fully exploit his victory due to factors affecting all combatants. The devastation inflicted by 25 years of warfare meant armies spent more time foraging than fighting, forcing them to become smaller and more mobile, with a much greater emphasis on cavalry. Difficulties in gathering provisions meant campaigns started later, and restricted them to areas that could be easily supplied, usually close to rivers.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009587-130) In addition, the French army in Germany was shattered at [Tuttlingen](/wiki/Battle_of_Tuttlingen "Battle of Tuttlingen") in November by Bavarian general [Franz von Mercy](/wiki/Franz_von_Mercy "Franz von Mercy").[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009643–645-131)

Soon after Rocroi, Ferdinand invited Sweden and France to attend peace talks in the [Westphalian](/wiki/Westphalia "Westphalia") towns of [Münster](/wiki/M%C3%BCnster "Münster") and [Osnabrück](/wiki/Osnabr%C3%BCck "Osnabrück"),[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009671-132) but negotiations were delayed when Christian of Denmark blockaded Hamburg and increased toll payments in the Baltic.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009687-133) This severely impacted the Dutch and Swedish economies, and in December 1643 the [Torstensson War](/wiki/Torstensson_War "Torstensson War") began when the Swedes invaded Jutland with Dutch naval support. Torstensson and the Swedes invaded Jutland with Dutch naval support and Horn launched an invasion through [Scania](/wiki/Scania "Scania"). Ferdinand assembled an army under Gallas to attack the Swedes from the rear and to support Denmark, which proved a disastrous decision. Leaving [Wrangel](/wiki/Carl_Gustaf_Wrangel "Carl Gustaf Wrangel") to finish the war in Denmark, in May 1644 Torstensson marched back into Germany. Gallas was unable to stop him, and the Danes sued for peace after their defeat at [Fehmarn](/wiki/Battle_of_Fehmarn_(1644) "Battle of Fehmarn (1644)") in October 1644.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938472–473-134)

In August 1644, the French and Bavarian armies met in the three day [Battle of Freiburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Freiburg "Battle of Freiburg"), in which both sides suffered heavy casualties. Convinced the war could no longer be won, Maximilian now put pressure on Ferdinand to end the conflict.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECroxton1998273-135) Shortly after peace talks restarted in November, Gallas' army disintegrated and the remnants retreated into Bohemia, where they were scattered by Torstensson at [Jankau](/wiki/Battle_of_Jankau "Battle of Jankau") in March 1645.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009693–695-136) In May, Bavarians under von Mercy destroyed a French detachment at [Herbsthausen](/wiki/Battle_of_Herbsthausen "Battle of Herbsthausen"), before he in turn was defeated and killed at [Second Nördlingen](/wiki/Battle_of_N%C3%B6rdlingen_(1645) "Battle of Nördlingen (1645)") in August.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonney200264-137) In September, John George of Saxony signed a six-month truce with Sweden, then agreed to remain neutral in the March 1646 Treaty of Eulenberg.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009711-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Slaget_vid_Prag_%281648%29%2C_ur_%22Theatri_Europ%C3%A6i...%22_1663_-_Skoklosters_slott_-_99875.tif/lossy-page1-330px-Slaget_vid_Prag_%281648%29%2C_ur_%22Theatri_Europ%C3%A6i...%22_1663_-_Skoklosters_slott_-_99875.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slaget_vid_Prag_(1648),_ur_%22Theatri_Europ%C3%A6i...%22_1663_-_Skoklosters_slott_-_99875.tif)

The final battle of the war; the Swedish [Siege of Prague](/wiki/Battle_of_Prague_(1648) "Battle of Prague (1648)") in 1648

Under [Turenne](/wiki/Henri_de_La_Tour_d%27Auvergne,_Viscount_of_Turenne "Henri de La Tour d'Auvergne, Viscount of Turenne"), French commander in the Rhineland, and Wrangel, who had replaced Torstensson, the French and Swedes separately invaded Bavaria in the summer of 1646.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938493–494-139) Maximilian was soon desperate to end the war he was largely responsible for starting, at which point the Spanish publicised a secret offer by Mazarin to exchange French-occupied Catalonia for the Spanish Netherlands. Angered by this duplicity, the Dutch agreed a truce with Spain in January 1647 and began to negotiate their own peace terms.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938495–496-140) Having failed to acquire the Netherlands through diplomacy, Mazarin decided to do so by force. To free up resources for the attempt, on 14 March 1647 he signed the [Truce of Ulm](/wiki/Truce_of_Ulm "Truce of Ulm") with Bavaria, [Cologne](/wiki/Electorate_of_Cologne "Electorate of Cologne") and Sweden.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009716-141)

The planned offensive fell apart when Turenne's mostly German troops mutinied, while Bavarian general [Johann von Werth](/wiki/Johann_von_Werth "Johann von Werth") refused to comply with the truce.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938496-142) Although the mutinies were quickly suppressed, Maximilian felt obliged to follow Werth's example, and in September ordered Bronckhorst-Gronsfeld to combine the remnants of the Bavarian army with Imperial troops under [Peter von Holzappel](/wiki/Peter_Melander,_Graf_von_Holzappel "Peter Melander, Graf von Holzappel").[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009726-143) Outnumbered by a Franco-Swedish army led by Wrangel and Turenne, they were defeated at [Zusmarshausen](/wiki/Battle_of_Zusmarshausen "Battle of Zusmarshausen") in May 1648. Holzappel was killed, and although most of his army escaped thanks to [Raimondo Montecuccoli](/wiki/Raimondo_Montecuccoli "Raimondo Montecuccoli"), Bavaria was left defenceless once again.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009740–741-144)

The Swedes sent a second force under [von Königsmarck](/wiki/Hans_Christoff_von_K%C3%B6nigsmarck "Hans Christoff von Königsmarck") to [attack Prague](/wiki/Battle_of_Prague_(1648) "Battle of Prague (1648)"), seizing the castle and [Malá Strana](/wiki/Mal%C3%A1_Strana "Malá Strana") district in July. The main objective was to gain as much loot as possible before the war ended; they failed to take the [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town_(Prague) "Old Town (Prague)") but captured treasures including the *[Codex Gigas](/wiki/Codex_Gigas "Codex Gigas")*, which can now be seen in Stockholm. When a Spanish offensive in Flanders ended with defeat at [Lens](/wiki/Battle_of_Lens "Battle of Lens") in August 1648, Ferdinand finally agreed terms and on 24 October, he signed peace treaties with France and Sweden, ending the war.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938501-145)

#### Italy and Switzerland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Italy and Switzerland")]

See also: [Bündner Wirren](/wiki/B%C3%BCndner_Wirren "Bündner Wirren"), [Valtellina War](/wiki/Valtellina_War "Valtellina War"), [War of the Mantuan Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Mantuan_Succession "War of the Mantuan Succession"), [Piedmontese Civil War](/wiki/Piedmontese_Civil_War "Piedmontese Civil War"), and [Neapolitan Revolt of 1647](/wiki/Neapolitan_Revolt_of_1647 "Neapolitan Revolt of 1647")

[![Thirty Years' War is located in Northern Italy](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/North_Italy_relief_location_map.png/500px-North_Italy_relief_location_map.png)](/wiki/File:North_Italy_relief_location_map.png "Thirty Years' War is located in Northern Italy")
