Pinerolo

Northern Italy

Control of Northern Italy provided access to the vulnerable southern borders of France and Austria. It also contained large sections of the [Spanish Road](/wiki/Spanish_Road "Spanish Road"), which allowed Spain to safely move recruits from Italy to support their war in the Netherlands. The French sought to disrupt this vital supply route by attacking the Spanish-held Duchy of Milan, or blocking the Alpine passes.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanlon2016118–119-146)

[Montferrat](/wiki/Duchy_of_Montferrat "Duchy of Montferrat") and its fortress of [Casale Monferrato](/wiki/Casale_Monferrato "Casale Monferrato") were subsidiary territories of the [Duchy of Mantua](/wiki/Duchy_of_Mantua "Duchy of Mantua") and their possession allowed the holder to threaten Milan. When the [Duke of Mantua](/wiki/Vincenzo_II_Gonzaga,_Duke_of_Mantua "Vincenzo II Gonzaga, Duke of Mantua") died in December 1627, France and Spain backed rival claimants, resulting in the 1628 to 1631 War of the Mantuan Succession.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938235–236-147) The French-born [Duke of Nevers](/wiki/Charles_I_Gonzaga,_Duke_of_Mantua "Charles I Gonzaga, Duke of Mantua") was backed by France and the Republic of Venice, his rival the [Duke of Guastalla](/wiki/Ferrante_II_Gonzaga,_Duke_of_Guastalla "Ferrante II Gonzaga, Duke of Guastalla") by Spain, Ferdinand II, Savoy and [Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany"). While a relatively minor conflict, the struggle had a disproportionate impact on the Thirty Years War, since [Pope Urban VIII](/wiki/Pope_Urban_VIII "Pope Urban VIII") viewed Habsburg expansion in Italy as a threat to the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"). His opposition to Ferdinand II divided the Catholic powers, and made it acceptable for France to employ Protestant allies against Austria.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938247-148)

In March 1629, the French stormed Savoyard positions in the Pas de Suse, lifted the Spanish siege of Casale, and captured [Pinerolo](/wiki/Pinerolo "Pinerolo").[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThion200862-149) The [Treaty of Suza](/wiki/Treaty_of_Suza "Treaty of Suza") then ceded the two fortresses to France and allowed their troops unrestricted passage through Savoyard territory, giving them control over [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") and the Alpine passes into southern France.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerretti201412–18-150) However, as soon as the main French army withdrew in late 1629, the Spanish and Savoyards besieged Casale once again. At the same time, mercenaries funded by Ferdinand II were used in a Spanish offensive which routed the main Venetian field army, and forced Nevers to abandon Mantua. By October 1630, the French position seemed so precarious their representatives agreed the Treaty of Ratisbon. It was never ratified, as Richelieu claimed he had not approved the terms.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938263–264-151)

Several factors restored the French position in northern Italy, notably a devastating outbreak of [plague](/wiki/1629%E2%80%931631_Italian_plague "1629–1631 Italian plague"); between 1629 and 1631, over 60,000 died in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and 46,000 in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), with proportionate losses elsewhere.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKohn1995200-152) Richelieu took advantage of the diversion of imperial resources to fund a [Swedish invasion of Germany](/wiki/Swedish_intervention_in_the_Thirty_Years%27_War "Swedish intervention in the Thirty Years' War"), whose success forced the Spanish-Savoyard alliance to withdraw from Casale and sign the Treaty of Cherasco in April 1631. Nevers was confirmed as Duke of Mantua and although Richelieu's representative, Cardinal Mazarin, agreed to evacuate Pinerolo, it was later secretly returned under an agreement with [Victor Amadeus I, Duke of Savoy](/wiki/Victor_Amadeus_I,_Duke_of_Savoy "Victor Amadeus I, Duke of Savoy"). With the exception of the 1639 to 1642 [Piedmontese Civil War](/wiki/Piedmontese_Civil_War "Piedmontese Civil War"), this secured the French position in northern Italy for the next twenty years.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerretti201420-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Dankaerts-Historis-9287.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Dankaerts-Historis-9287.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dankaerts-Historis-9287.tif)

Siege and capture of [Casale Monferrato](/wiki/Casale_Monferrato "Casale Monferrato") by French troops, 1630

After the outbreak of the Franco-Spanish War in 1635, Richelieu supported a renewed offensive by Victor Amadeus against Milan to tie down Spanish resources. These included an unsuccessful attack on [Valenza](/wiki/Valenza "Valenza") in 1635, plus minor victories at [Tornavento](/wiki/Battle_of_Tornavento "Battle of Tornavento") and [Mombaldone](/wiki/Mombaldone "Mombaldone").[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy1995125-154) However, the anti-Habsburg alliance in northern Italy fell apart when first Charles of Mantua died in September 1637, then Victor Amadeus in October, whose death led to a struggle for control of the Savoyard state between his widow [Christine of France](/wiki/Christine_of_France "Christine of France") and brothers, [Thomas](/wiki/Thomas_Francis,_Prince_of_Carignano "Thomas Francis, Prince of Carignano") and [Maurice](/wiki/Prince_Maurice_of_Savoy "Prince Maurice of Savoy").[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009259-155)

In 1639, their quarrel erupted into open warfare, with France backing Christine and Spain the two brothers, and resulted in the [Siege of Turin](/wiki/Siege_of_Turin_(1640) "Siege of Turin (1640)"). One of the most famous military events of the 17th century, at one stage it featured no less than three different armies besieging each other. However, revolts in Portugal and Catalonia forced the Spanish to cease operations in Italy, and the war was settled on terms favourable to Christine and France.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanlon2016124-156)

In 1647, a French-backed rebellion succeeded in temporarily overthrowing Spanish rule in [Naples](/wiki/Neapolitan_Republic_(1647) "Neapolitan Republic (1647)"). The Spanish quickly crushed the [Neapolitan Revolt of 1647](/wiki/Neapolitan_Revolt_of_1647 "Neapolitan Revolt of 1647") and restored their rule over all of [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"), defeating multiple French expeditionary forces sent to back the rebels.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamen2003406-157) However, it exposed the weakness of Spanish rule in Italy and the alienation of the local elites from Madrid. In 1650, the governor of Milan wrote that as well as widespread dissatisfaction in the south, the only one of the Italian states that could be relied on was the [Duchy of Parma](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma "Duchy of Parma").[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamen2003407-158)

#### Iberian Peninsula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Iberian Peninsula")]

Main articles: [Reapers' War](/wiki/Reapers%27_War "Reapers' War") and [Portuguese Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Philip_II%27s_realms_in_1598.png/250px-Philip_II%27s_realms_in_1598.png)](/wiki/File:Philip_II%27s_realms_in_1598.png)

The [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union"); Spain's inability to protect Portuguese interests in the 1602 to 1663 [Dutch–Portuguese War](/wiki/Dutch%E2%80%93Portuguese_War "Dutch–Portuguese War") was a key factor in the 1640 [Portuguese Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War")

Throughout the 1630s, tax increases levied to pay for the war led to protests throughout Spanish territories, which in 1640 resulted in revolts: first in Portugal with the [Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War") to re-gain their independence against the [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union"),[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta2005-159) then in the [Principality of Catalonia](/wiki/Principality_of_Catalonia "Principality of Catalonia"). Both were backed by France as part of Richelieu's 'war by diversion', in January 1641 the rebels proclaimed a [Catalan Republic](/wiki/Catalan_Republic_(1641) "Catalan Republic (1641)").[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997153-160) The Madrid government quickly assembled an army of 26,000 men to crush the [Catalan revolt](/wiki/Reapers%27_War "Reapers' War"), which defeated the rebels at [Martorell](/wiki/Battle_of_Martorell_(1641) "Battle of Martorell (1641)") on 23 January 1641. The French now persuaded the [Catalan Courts](/wiki/Catalan_Courts "Catalan Courts") to recognise Louis XIII as [Count of Barcelona](/wiki/Count_of_Barcelona "Count of Barcelona"), and ruler of Catalonia.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Gelderen2002284-118)

On 26 January, a combined French-Catalan force routed a larger Spanish army at [Montjuïc](/wiki/Battle_of_Montju%C3%AFc_(1641) "Battle of Montjuïc (1641)") and secured [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"). However, the rebels soon found the new French administration differed little from the old, turning the war into a three-sided contest between the Franco-Catalan elite, the rural peasantry, and the Spanish. There was little serious fighting after France [took control of Perpignan](/wiki/Siege_of_Perpignan_(1642) "Siege of Perpignan (1642)") and [Roussillon](/wiki/Roussillon "Roussillon"), establishing the current-day Franco-Spanish border in the Pyrenees. The revolt ended in 1651 when Madrid [recaptured Barcelona](/wiki/Siege_of_Barcelona_(1651) "Siege of Barcelona (1651)").[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell2005431–448-161)

### Peace of Westphalia (1648)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Peace of Westphalia (1648)")]

Main article: [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Holy_Roman_Empire_1648.svg/250px-Holy_Roman_Empire_1648.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Holy_Roman_Empire_1648.svg)

[Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") after the Peace of Westphalia, 1648

In its final form, the Peace of Westphalia consisted of three separate agreements. These were the [Peace of Münster](/wiki/Peace_of_M%C3%BCnster "Peace of Münster") between Spain and the Dutch Republic, the Treaty of Osnabrück between the Empire and Sweden, and the Treaty of Münster between the Empire and France. Split between Münster and Osnabrück, talks began in 1642, with a total of 109 delegations attending at one time or other. After the Swedes rejected Christian of Denmark as mediator, the negotiators finally agreed on [Papal Legate](/wiki/Papal_Legate "Papal Legate") [Fabio Chigi](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VII "Pope Alexander VII"), and Venetian envoy [Alvise Contarini](/wiki/Alvise_Contarini_(diplomat) "Alvise Contarini (diplomat)").[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECroxton20133–4-162)

The first to be signed on 30 January 1648, the Peace of Münster forms part of the Westphalia settlement since the Dutch Republic was still considered imperial territory. It confirmed Dutch independence from Spain, although the Republic was not officially acknowledged as being outside the Empire until 1728.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009746-163) The Dutch also gained a monopoly over trade conducted through the Scheldt estuary, ensuring the commercial ascendancy of [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"). [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), capital of the Spanish Netherlands and previously the most important port in northern Europe, would not recover economically until the late 19th century.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995a197–199-164)

The terms of the separate treaties with France and Sweden had first to be agreed by Ferdinand and the Imperial Estates. It has been argued they were a "major turning point in German and European...legal history", because they went beyond normal peace settlements and effected major constitutional and religious changes to the Empire itself.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELesaffer199771-165) Negotiations were complex and slow, with states like Saxony and Bavaria having very different views on desired outcomes. Ferdinand delayed signing, hoping for an improvement in his military position, but with Swedish troops on the verge of taking Prague, he finally did so on 24 October.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938500–501-166)[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009746–747-167)

Key elements of the Peace were provisions confirming the autonomy of states within the Empire, Ferdinand's acceptance of the supremacy of the Imperial Diet, and those seeking to prevent future religious conflict. Article 5 reconfirmed the Augsburg settlement, established 1624 as the basis, or "Normaljahr", for determining the dominant religion of a state and guaranteed freedom of worship for religious minorities. Article 7 recognised Calvinism as a Reformed faith and removed the *ius reformandi*, the requirement that if a ruler changed his religion, his subjects had to follow suit. These terms did not apply to the hereditary lands of the Habsburg monarchy, such as Lower and Upper Austria.[[150]](#cite_note-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Westfaelischer_Friede_in_Muenster_%28Gerard_Terborch_1648%29.jpg/250px-Westfaelischer_Friede_in_Muenster_%28Gerard_Terborch_1648%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westfaelischer_Friede_in_Muenster_(Gerard_Terborch_1648).jpg)

Signing of the [Peace of Münster](/wiki/Peace_of_M%C3%BCnster "Peace of Münster") between [Spain](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") and the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), 30 January 1648

In terms of territorial concessions, Brandenburg-Prussia received [Farther Pomerania](/wiki/Farther_Pomerania "Farther Pomerania"), and the bishoprics of Magdeburg, Halberstadt, [Kammin](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Kammin "Bishopric of Kammin"), and [Minden](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Minden "Bishopric of Minden"). Frederick's son [Charles Louis](/wiki/Charles_I_Louis,_Elector_Palatine "Charles I Louis, Elector Palatine") regained the Lower Palatinate and became the eighth imperial elector, although Bavaria kept the Upper Palatinate and its electoral vote.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009746-163) In Lorraine, the [Three Bishoprics](/wiki/Three_Bishoprics "Three Bishoprics") of [Metz](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Metz "Prince-Bishopric of Metz"), [Toul](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Toul "Prince-Bishopric of Toul") and [Verdun](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Verdun "Prince-Bishopric of Verdun"), occupied by France since 1552, were formally ceded, as were the cities of the [Décapole](/wiki/D%C3%A9capole "Décapole") in Alsace, with the exception of [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") and [Mulhouse](/wiki/Mulhouse "Mulhouse").[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009711-138) Sweden received an indemnity of five million [thalers](/wiki/Thalers "Thalers"), the imperial territories of [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania"), and the Prince-bishoprics of Bremen and Verden, which also gave them a seat in the Imperial Diet.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009707-169)

The terms were denounced by [Pope Innocent X](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_X "Pope Innocent X"), for whom the bishoprics ceded to France and Brandenburg were property of the Catholic church, and thus his to assign.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyan1948597-170) It also disappointed many exiles by accepting Catholicism as the dominant religion in Bohemia, Upper and Lower Austria, all Protestant strongholds prior to 1618. Fighting did not end immediately, since demobilising over 200,000 soldiers took time, and the last Swedish garrison did not leave Germany until 1654.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938504-171) In addition, Mazarin insisted on excluding the [Burgundian Circle](/wiki/Burgundian_Circle "Burgundian Circle") from the Treaty of Münster, allowing France to continue its campaign against Spain in the Low Countries, a war that continued until the 1659 [Treaty of the Pyrenees](/wiki/Treaty_of_the_Pyrenees "Treaty of the Pyrenees"). The [political disintegration](/wiki/Deluge_(history) "Deluge (history)") of Poland-Lithuania led to the 1655 to 1660 [Second Northern War](/wiki/Second_Northern_War "Second Northern War") with Sweden, which also involved Denmark, Russia and Brandenburg, while two Swedish attempts to impose its control on the port of [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen") failed in [1654 and 1666](/wiki/Swedish_Wars_on_Bremen "Swedish Wars on Bremen").[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009757-172)

It has been argued the Peace established the principle known as [Westphalian sovereignty](/wiki/Westphalian_sovereignty "Westphalian sovereignty"), the idea of non-interference in domestic affairs by outside powers, although this has since been challenged. The "Congress" model was used for negotiations at [Aix-la-Chapelle in 1668](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aix-la-Chapelle_(1668) "Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1668)"), [Nijmegen in 1678](/wiki/Treaties_of_Nijmegen "Treaties of Nijmegen"), and [Ryswick](/wiki/Peace_of_Ryswick "Peace of Ryswick") in 1697, although unlike the 19th-century system, these were intended to end wars, rather than prevent them.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECroxton2013331–332-173)

## Human and financial cost of the war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Human and financial cost of the war")]

See also: [Second plague pandemic](/wiki/Second_plague_pandemic "Second plague pandemic")

The Thirty Years' War is part of what historians sometimes call "[The General Crisis](/wiki/The_General_Crisis "The General Crisis")" of the mid-17th century, a period of sustained conflict and unrest in areas ranging from [Ming China](/wiki/Ming_China "Ming China") to the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), [Tsarist Russia](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia") and the Holy Roman Empire. In each of these, fighting combined with [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine") and disease to inflict severe losses on local civilian populations.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker20081053-174) While the war certainly ranks as one of the worst of these events, 19th-century [German nationalists](/wiki/German_nationalism "German nationalism") often exaggerated its impact to illustrate the dangers of a divided Germany.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938510-175)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Bev%C3%B6lkerkungsr%C3%BCckgang_im_HRRDN_nach_dem_Drei%C3%9Figj%C3%A4hrigen_Krieg.PNG/250px-Bev%C3%B6lkerkungsr%C3%BCckgang_im_HRRDN_nach_dem_Drei%C3%9Figj%C3%A4hrigen_Krieg.PNG)](/wiki/File:Bev%C3%B6lkerkungsr%C3%BCckgang_im_HRRDN_nach_dem_Drei%C3%9Figj%C3%A4hrigen_Krieg.PNG)

[Population decline](/wiki/Population_decline "Population decline") within Germany, 1618 to 1648   
 **Note**: *Decline* includes factors such as emigration from rural to more secure urban areas and does not equate to *deaths*

33–66%

>66%

Claims of up to 12 million deaths from a population of 18 million are no longer considered realistic, while upper estimates of material losses are not supported by contemporary evidence, or in some cases exceed prewar tax records.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997188–189-176) Regardless, modern commentators agree the war was a man-made mortality disaster previously unknown in Europe. Estimates of total deaths range from 4.5 to 8 million, most incurred after 1630 when Sweden entered the war, the vast majority being civilians.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutram2001156–159-177)

Battles generally featured armies of around 13,000 to 20,000 each, one of the largest being Alte Veste in 1632 with a combined 70,000 to 85,000. Estimates of total combatants deployed by both sides within Germany range from an average of 80,000 to 100,000 from 1618 to 1626, peaking at 250,000 in 1632 and falling to under 160,000 by 1648.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClodfelter200840-9)

Casualty rates for those who served in the military could be extremely high. Of 230 men conscripted from the Swedish village of [Bygdeå](/wiki/Bygde%C3%A5 "Bygdeå") between 1621 and 1639, 215 are recorded as dead or missing, while another 5 returned home crippled.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997173-19) Historian Peter Wilson puts those killed or wounded in action at around 450,000. Research shows disease increased that number by a factor of between two and three, which suggests total military casualties ranged from 1.3 to 1.8 million.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009791-17) Although his methodology has been disputed, sociologist [Pitirim Sorokin](/wiki/Pitirim_Sorokin "Pitirim Sorokin") estimates 2,071,000 military deaths, of which 1,151,000 occurred between 1635 and 1648.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy198388–91-178)

Local returns show only 3% of civilian deaths were due to military action, the major causes being starvation (12%), [bubonic plague](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague") (64%), [typhus](/wiki/Typhus "Typhus") (4%), and [dysentery](/wiki/Dysentery "Dysentery") (5%).[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutram2001160–161-179) Poor harvests throughout the 1630s and repeated plundering of the same areas led to widespread famine, with reports of people eating grass, too weak to accept [alms](/wiki/Alms "Alms"), or resorting to [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism").[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009345-180) Although regular outbreaks of disease were common prior to 1618, their spread was accelerated by the influx of foreign soldiers, the shifting locations of battle fronts, and displacement of rural populations into already crowded cities.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutram2002250-181) Soldiers transferred from Germany allegedly sparked the [1629–1631 Italian plague](/wiki/1629%E2%80%931631_Italian_plague "1629–1631 Italian plague"), described as the "worst mortality crisis to affect Italy during the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period")".[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlfaniPercoco20191175-182) This resulted in some 280,000 deaths, with estimates of up to a million.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHays2005103-183)

Modern historians generally agree that the German areas of the Holy Roman Empire experienced an overall [population decline](/wiki/Population_decline "Population decline") of roughly 40%, from 18 and 20 million in 1600 to between 11 and 13 million in 1650, and did not regain pre-war levels until 1750.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker20081058-184)[[167]](#cite_note-185) Nearly 50% of these losses were incurred during the first period of Swedish intervention from 1630 to 1635. [The Czech lands](/wiki/Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown "Lands of the Bohemian Crown") also saw a 27% population decline, from approximately 2.95 million in 1600 to 2.15 million in 1650.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009788-186) The high mortality rate was partly due to the reliance of all sides on foreign mercenaries, often unpaid and required to live off the land.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997122-187) Lack of a sense of 'shared community' resulted in atrocities such as the destruction of Magdeburg, in turn creating large numbers of refugees who were extremely susceptible to sickness and hunger. While flight saved lives in the short-term, in the long run it often proved catastrophic.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutram2002245–246-188)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Vrancx_Soldiers_Plundering.jpg/330px-Vrancx_Soldiers_Plundering.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vrancx_Soldiers_Plundering.jpg)

Soldiers plundering a farm

In 1940, historian [Günther Franz](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Franz "Günther Franz") published an analysis of data from across Germany covering the period from 1618 to 1648. Broadly confirmed by more recent work, he concluded about 40% of the civilian rural population became casualties, and 33% of the urban.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutram2002248-23) These figures need to be read with care, since Franz calculated the *absolute decline* in pre and post-war populations, or 'total demographic loss'. They therefore include factors unrelated to death or disease, such as permanent migration to areas outside the Empire or lower birthrates, a common but less obvious impact of extended warfare.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutram2001152-189)[[s]](#cite_note-190) There were also wide regional variations, with areas of northwest Germany experiencing minimal loss of population, while those of Mecklenburg, [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania") and [Württemberg](/wiki/Duchy_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Duchy of Württemberg") fell by nearly 50%.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997188–189-176)

Although some towns may have overstated their losses to avoid taxes, individual records confirm serious declines; from 1620 to 1650, the population of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") fell from 22,000 to 17,000, that of [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg") from 48,000 to 21,000.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938512-191) The financial impact is less clear; while the war caused short-term economic dislocation, especially in the period [1618 to 1623](/wiki/Kipper_und_Wipper "Kipper und Wipper"), overall it accelerated existing changes in trading patterns. It does not appear to have reversed ongoing macro-economic trends, such as the reduction of price differentials between regional markets, and a greater degree of market integration across Europe.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchulzeVolckart201930-192) The death toll may have improved living standards for the survivors; one study shows wages in Germany increased by 40% in real terms between 1603 and 1652.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPfisterRiedelUebele201218-193)

## Military developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Military developments")]

Innovations made by Gustavus in particular are considered part of the evolution known as the "[Military Revolution](/wiki/Military_Revolution "Military Revolution")", although whether tactics or technology were at the heart of these changes is still debated.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharman2018493–495-194) Introduced by [Maurice of Orange](/wiki/Maurice,_Prince_of_Orange "Maurice, Prince of Orange") in the 1590s, these sought to increase infantry firepower by moving from massed [columns](/wiki/Column_(formation) "Column (formation)") to [line formation](/wiki/Line_(formation) "Line (formation)"). Gustavus further reduced the ten ranks used by Maurice to six, and increased the proportion of [musketeers](/wiki/Musketeer "Musketeer") to [pikemen](/wiki/Pike_(weapon) "Pike (weapon)"). He also enhanced their firepower by providing each unit with quick-firing light artillery pieces on either flank. The best example of their application in battle was the victory over Tilly's traditionally organised army at Breitenfeld in September 1631.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997185-195)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Firing_Breitenfeld_formations.jpg/330px-Firing_Breitenfeld_formations.jpg)](/wiki/File:Firing_Breitenfeld_formations.jpg)

[Breitenfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Breitenfeld_(1631) "Battle of Breitenfeld (1631)") 1631; Tilly's army *(left)* are deployed two companies deep, the Swedes *(right)* just one company deep.

Line formations were often harder to co-ordinate, as demonstrated when the supposedly obsolete Spanish [tercios](/wiki/Tercios "Tercios") defeated the Swedes at [Nördlingen](/wiki/Battle_of_N%C3%B6rdlingen_(1634) "Battle of Nördlingen (1634)") in 1634.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1976200-196) Since they also lacked the offensive impact of columns, Gustavus compensated by requiring his cavalry to be far more aggressive, often employing Finnish *[Hakkapeliitta](/wiki/Hakkapeliitta "Hakkapeliitta")* as shock troops. However, even the Swedes used columns on occasion, notably the failed assault at Alte Veste in September 1632. The line versus column debate continued into the early 19th century, and both were employed during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler1990130–137-197)

Such tactics needed professional soldiers, who could retain formation, reload and fire disciplined [salvos](/wiki/Salvo "Salvo") while under attack, as well as the use of standardised weapons. The first half of the 17th century saw the publication of numerous instruction manuals showing the movements required, thirty-two for pikemen and forty-two for musketeers.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1976202-198) In theory, it took up to six months to train infantry to operate in this way, but in reality many went into battle with far less experience.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997184-199) It also placed greater responsibility on junior officers who provided the vital links between senior commanders and the tactical unit. One of the first military schools designed to produce such men was set up at [Siegen](/wiki/Siegen "Siegen") in 1616, and others soon followed.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997184-199)

On the other hand, strategic thinking failed to develop at the same pace. Historian [Jeremy Black](/wiki/Jeremy_Black_(historian) "Jeremy Black (historian)") claims most campaigns were "inconclusive", since they were primarily concerned with gaining access to supplies and money for the soldiers, rather than focused strategic objectives. The disconnect between military and diplomatic goals helps explain why the war lasted so long, and peace proved so elusive.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECroxton1998254-200) When fighting officially ended in 1648, there were still over 150,000 troops under arms within the Empire, small numbers by modern standards, but unprecedented at the time.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009770-201) Most 17th century states could not finance armies of this size for extended periods, forcing them to depend on "contributions" from areas they passed through.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997177-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Rocroi%2C_el_%C3%BAltimo_tercio%2C_por_Augusto_Ferrer-Dalmau.jpg/250px-Rocroi%2C_el_%C3%BAltimo_tercio%2C_por_Augusto_Ferrer-Dalmau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rocroi,_el_%C3%BAltimo_tercio,_por_Augusto_Ferrer-Dalmau.jpg)

The [Battle of Rocroi](/wiki/Battle_of_Rocroi "Battle of Rocroi") (1643) is often seen as the end of the battlefield supremacy of the *[tercios](/wiki/Tercio "Tercio")*.

Supplies thus became the limiting factor in planning campaigns, a problem that grew more acute as the war progressed. Armies became smaller, with a greater emphasis on cavalry that could cover greater distances and move faster, rather than slow moving infantry. Poor infrastructure also required commanders to stay close to rivers, then the primary means of bulk transportation, and meant they could not move too far from their main bases.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECroxton1998255–256-203) Feeding the troops became an objective in itself, unconnected to diplomatic goals and largely uncontrolled by their central governments. The result was "armies increasingly devoid of intelligible political objectives...degenerating into travelling armed mobs, living in a symbiotic relationship with the countryside they passed through".[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Connell1990147-204) This often conflicted with the political aims of their employers; the devastation inflicted in 1628 and 1629 by imperial troops on Brandenburg and Saxony, both nominally allies, was a major factor in their support for Swedish intervention.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938257–258-205)

Finally, some commentators argue that while the Thirty Years' War certainly played a role in accelerating the development of new tactics and technology, the need to fund, supply, and direct permanent armies for extended periods across wide-ranging theaters was of greater significance. This required much more sophisticated mechanisms and led to the transfer of organised violence from "contractor" generals like Wallenstein or Mansfeld to nation-states. In this sense, the truly "revolutionary" aspect of the Military Revolution was less about the tactics and technology used by soldiers and more about the institutions required to support them.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackstrom2023246–248-206)

## Social and cultural impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Social and cultural impact")]

The breakdown of social order caused by the war was often more significant and longer lasting than the immediate damage.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938516-207) The collapse of local government created landless peasants, who banded together to protect themselves from the soldiers of both sides, and led to widespread rebellions in [Upper Austria](/wiki/Peasants%27_War_in_Upper_Austria "Peasants' War in Upper Austria"), Bavaria and Brandenburg. Soldiers devastated one area before moving on, leaving large tracts of land empty of people and changing the ecosystem. Food shortages were worsened by an explosion in the rodent population, while Bavaria was overrun by wolves in the winter of 1638, and its crops destroyed by packs of wild pigs the following spring.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009784-208)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Bondi_brennandi_hus.jpg/250px-Bondi_brennandi_hus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bondi_brennandi_hus.jpg)

A peasant begs for mercy in front of his burning farm; by the 1630s, being caught in the open by soldiers from either side was "tantamount to a death sentence".[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOutram2002250-181)

Contemporaries spoke of a "frenzy of despair" as people sought to make sense of the relentless and often random bloodshed unleashed by the war. Attributed by religious authorities to divine retribution, attempts to identify a supernatural cause led to a series of [witch-hunts](/wiki/Witch-hunt "Witch-hunt"), starting in Franconia in 1626, then quickly spreading to other parts of Germany.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2012220-209) They began in the [Bishopric of Würzburg](/wiki/Bishopric_of_W%C3%BCrzburg "Bishopric of Würzburg"), an area with a history of such events going back to 1616 and now re-ignited by [Bishop von Ehrenberg](/wiki/Philipp_Adolf_von_Ehrenberg "Philipp Adolf von Ehrenberg"), a devout Catholic eager to assert the church's authority in his territories. By the time he died in 1631, [over 900 people](/wiki/W%C3%BCrzburg_witch_trial "Würzburg witch trial") from all levels of society had been [executed](/wiki/Burned_at_the_stake "Burned at the stake").[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJensen200793-210)

The [Bamberg witch trials](/wiki/Bamberg_witch_trials "Bamberg witch trials"), held in the nearby [Bishopric of Bamberg](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Bamberg "Bishopric of Bamberg") from 1626 to 1631, claimed over one thousand lives; in 1629, 274 died in the [Eichstätt witch trials](/wiki/Eichst%C3%A4tt_witch_trials "Eichstätt witch trials"), plus another 50 in the adjacent Duchy of [Palatinate-Neuburg](/wiki/Palatinate-Neuburg "Palatinate-Neuburg").[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrevor-Roper200183–117-211) Elsewhere, persecution followed imperial military success, expanding into [Baden](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Baden "Margraviate of Baden") and the Palatinate following their reconquest by Tilly, then into the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland").[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1996163-212) However, the extent to which they were symptomatic of the impact of the conflict on society is debatable, since many took place in areas relatively untouched by the war. Concerned their brutality would discredit the Counter-Reformation, Ferdinand ensured active persecution largely ended by 1630.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1996171–172-213)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Sebastiaan_Vrancx_%28studio%29_-_A_landscape_with_travellers_ambushed_outside_a_small_town.jpg/250px-Sebastiaan_Vrancx_%28studio%29_-_A_landscape_with_travellers_ambushed_outside_a_small_town.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sebastiaan_Vrancx_(studio)_-_A_landscape_with_travellers_ambushed_outside_a_small_town.jpg)

Local people attacking [mercenaries](/wiki/Mercenary "Mercenary") and their entourage outside a small town, by [Sebastiaen Vrancx](/wiki/Sebastiaen_Vrancx "Sebastiaen Vrancx")

Although the war caused immense destruction, it has also been credited with sparking a revival in German literature, including the creation of societies dedicated to "purging foreign elements" from the German language.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriehs-214) One example is *[Simplicius Simplicissimus](/wiki/Simplicius_Simplicissimus "Simplicius Simplicissimus")*, a [picaresque novel](/wiki/Picaresque_novel "Picaresque novel") written by [Hans Jakob Christoffel von Grimmelshausen](/wiki/Hans_Jakob_Christoffel_von_Grimmelshausen "Hans Jakob Christoffel von Grimmelshausen") in 1668, providing a realistic portrayal of a soldier's life based on his own experience, which is verified by other sources.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETalbott20213–4-215) Other less famous examples include the diaries of [Peter Hagendorf](/wiki/Peter_Hagendorf "Peter Hagendorf"), a participant in the [Sack of Magdeburg](/wiki/Sack_of_Magdeburg "Sack of Magdeburg") whose descriptions of the everyday brutalities of the war remain compelling.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHelfferich2009283–284-216)

For German, and to a lesser extent Czech writers, the war was remembered as a defining moment of national trauma, the 18th century poet and playwright [Friedrich Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller") being one of many to use it in their work. Variously known as the 'Great German War,' 'Great War', or 'Great Schism', for 19th- and early 20th-century German nationalists it showed the dangers of a divided Germany and was used to justify the creation of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") in 1871, as well as the [Greater Germanic Reich](/wiki/Greater_Germanic_Reich "Greater Germanic Reich") envisaged by the [Nazis](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECramer200718–19-217) [Bertolt Brecht](/wiki/Bertolt_Brecht "Bertolt Brecht") used it as the backdrop for his 1939 anti-war play *[Mother Courage and Her Children](/wiki/Mother_Courage_and_Her_Children "Mother Courage and Her Children")*, while its enduring cultural resonance is illustrated by the novel *[Tyll](/wiki/Tyll_(novel) "Tyll (novel)")*; written by Austro-German author [Daniel Kehlmann](/wiki/Daniel_Kehlmann "Daniel Kehlmann") and also set during the war, it was nominated for the 2020 [Booker Prize](/wiki/Booker_Prize "Booker Prize").[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETalbott20216-218)

## Political consequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Political consequences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Europe_map_1648.PNG/250px-Europe_map_1648.PNG)](/wiki/File:Europe_map_1648.PNG)

Europe after the Peace of Westphalia, 1648

The Peace of Westphalia reconfirmed "German liberties", ending Habsburg attempts to convert the Holy Roman Empire into a centralised state similar to Spain. Over the next 50 years, Bavaria, Brandenburg-Prussia, Saxony, and others increasingly pursued their own policies, while Sweden gained a permanent foothold in the Empire. Despite these setbacks, the Habsburg lands suffered less from the war than many others. They also became a far more coherent geographical bloc with the addition of Bohemia, and the restoration of Catholicism throughout their territories.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMurdie201465-219)

By laying the foundations of the modern [nation state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state"), the Peace of Westphalia changed the relationship between rulers and ruled, many of whom previously had multiple political and religious allegiances. After 1648, they were now understood to be subject first and foremost to the laws and edicts of their respective state authority, not the claims of any other entity, either religious or secular. This made it easier to levy national forces loyal to the state and its leader; one lesson learned from Wallenstein and the Swedish invasion was the need for their own permanent armies, and German society became far more militarised.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonney200289–90-220)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Pomerania_1653.PNG/250px-Pomerania_1653.PNG)](/wiki/File:Pomerania_1653.PNG)

Swedish sovereignty over [Western Pomerania](/wiki/Western_Pomerania "Western Pomerania") (in blue) was confirmed in 1653, and finally ended only in 1815.

For Sweden, the war had established the kingdom as a major force in Northern Europe, but the direct benefits of the Peace of Westphalia ultimately proved short-lived. Unlike French gains, which were incorporated into France, Swedish territories remained part of the Empire, and they became members of the Lower and Upper Saxon *kreis*. While this provided both seats and influence in the Imperial Diet, it also brought Sweden into direct conflict with Brandenburg-Prussia and Saxony, their competitors in Pomerania. The income from their German possessions was relatively minor, and although parts of Pomerania remained Swedish until 1815, much of it was ceded to Prussia in 1679 and 1720.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMurdie201467–68-221)

France arguably gained more from the conflict than any other power, and by 1648, most of Richelieu's objectives had been achieved. These included separation of the Spanish and Austrian Habsburgs, expansion of the French frontier into the Holy Roman Empire, and an end to Spanish military supremacy in Northern Europe.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee200167–68-222) Although the Franco-Spanish war continued until 1659, the Peace of Westphalia allowed Louis XIV to begin replacing Spain as the predominant European power.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStorrs20066–7-223)

While religion remained a divisive political issue in many countries, the Thirty Years' War is arguably the last major European conflict where it was a primary driver. Future religious conflicts were either internal, such as the [Camisards](/wiki/Camisards "Camisards") revolt in southern France, or relatively minor, like the 1712 [Toggenburg War](/wiki/Toggenburg_War "Toggenburg War").[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutmann1988752–754-224) The war created the outlines of a Europe that persisted until 1815 and beyond, most significantly the nation-state of France, along with the start of a split between Germany and a separate Austro-Hungarian bloc.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcMurdie201467–68-221)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thirty_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** States that fought against the emperor at some point between 1618 and 1635.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Since officers were paid for each man "present", the numbers *Reported* frequently differed from *Actual*, or those available for duty. Variances between *Reported* and *Actual* are estimated as averaging up to 25% for the Dutch, 35% for the French and 50% for the Spanish.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParrott20018-4) Most battles of the period were fought between opposing forces of 13,000 to 20,000 men, so the numbers reflect *Maximum* at any one time, and exclude citizen militia, who often formed a large proportion of garrisons.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
3. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** These figures show numbers "In Service of", rather than ethnicity, since all armies were multinational. Most soldiers on both sides were German, but included large numbers of other nationalities. Based on an analysis of a mass grave discovered in 2011, a high proportion of "Swedish" forces at [Lützen](/wiki/Battle_of_L%C3%BCtzen_(1632) "Battle of Lützen (1632)") were ethnic Germans, while less than 50% even came from [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicklischRamsthalerMellerOthers2017-6)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** 1640 figures for the [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders"), when it was at its maximum strength; these are Reported numbers, so as mentioned elsewhere, the actual number of soldiers would have been considerably lower.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2004231-14) The Spanish army officially had more than 200,000 soldiers in 1640, but most were second line troops in garrisons elsewhere in Europe, not facing the Dutch.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClodfelter200839-15)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** Wilson estimates a total of 450,000 combat deaths on all sides, the vast majority of whom were German. By one calculation, four times as many Germans died fighting for Sweden as Swedes, and so casualties are referred to as being "In service", rather than by nationality.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009791-17)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** France lost another 200,000 to 300,000 killed or wounded in the related [Franco-Spanish War](/wiki/Franco-Spanish_War_(1635%E2%80%931659) "Franco-Spanish War (1635–1659)").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009790-20)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** Includes military losses from disease, which Wilson estimates as two to three for every one killed in combat.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009791-17)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-25)** [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Dreißigjähriger Krieg*, pronounced [[ˈdʁaɪ̯sɪçˌjɛːʁɪɡɐ kʁiːk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Drei%C3%9Figj%C3%A4hriger_Krieg.ogg "File:De-Dreißigjähriger Krieg.ogg")
9. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** Some commentators argue it began with the [War of the Jülich Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_J%C3%BClich_Succession "War of the Jülich Succession") in 1609.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-30)** One major impact of the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") was to transfer lands and influence from the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") to secular, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") rulers
11. **[^](#cite_ref-34)** As below, the acquisition of territories within the Empire by [Denmark-Norway](/wiki/Denmark-Norway "Denmark-Norway") and [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire") gave them an ongoing opportunity to intervene.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayden19731–23-33)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-36)** Although technically there were nearly 1,800 separate estates, only [300](/wiki/List_of_Imperial_Diet_participants_(1792) "List of Imperial Diet participants (1792)") were represented in the Imperial Diet or Circles. Most of the remaining 1,500 were [imperial Knights](/wiki/Imperial_Knight "Imperial Knight"), or individual members of the lower nobility, who were excluded.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson200921-35)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-37)** Its official title remains *Freie und Hansestadt Hamburg*
14. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** The [lands](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") ruled by the Austrian Habsburgs were often collectively known as "Austria"
15. **[^](#cite_ref-51)** Since electoral votes were associated with the position, rather than the individual, becoming king of Bohemia in addition to Elector Palatine would give Frederick two votes
16. **[^](#cite_ref-75)** As well as being brother-in-law to Frederick of the Palatinate, James I was also linked to Christian IV of Denmark, having married his elder sister [Anne of Denmark](/wiki/Anne_of_Denmark "Anne of Denmark") (1574–1619).[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009382-74)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-109)** While the death of Gustavus was greeted with dismay by most European Protestants, Richelieu was more ambivalent. The two were increasingly at odds over strategic objectives, although there is no evidence for contemporary claims he was involved in the king's death.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1938328-108)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-120)** Not to be confused with Freiberg in Saxony.
19. **[^](#cite_ref-190)** For example, the population of [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg") fell from 48,000 in 1620 to 21,000 in 1650, which Franz portrays as a demographic loss of 27,000; however, many of these were not dead, but emigrated due to decline in trade
